coredata.dc:title: Construction of fluorescent sensor array and three-dimensional microfluidic paper based analytical device for specific identification and visual determination of antibiotics in food
coredata.dc:creator: Li, Tingting
Guo, Guoqiang
Xing, Haoming
Tang, Siyuan
Hu, Houwen
Wang, Linfan
Qian, Xiaoqing
Chen, Da
coredata.dc:description: For antibiotics misuse since the global outbreak of COVID 19, a novel strategy for discriminating and detecting antibiotics is proposed based on the graphene quantum dots with multi-doped heteroatoms including F, N and P (M-GQDs), which exhibit blue emission (419.0 nm) under the excitation of 336.0 nm. Specifically, the fluorescence of M-GQDs is quenched by tetracyclines (TCs) owing to inner filter effect (IFE) and enhanced by alkane-modified fluoroquinolones (AFQs), which is attributed to restricted conformational rotation based on π-π stacking, hydrogen-bonding and electrostatic interactions. Meanwhile, the electron-accepting property of oxazine ring in oxazine-modified fluoroquinolones (OFQs) increases emission peak at 498.0 nm and decreases emission peak at 419.0 nm as the color changes from blue to cyan. Moreover, a cascade system integrated with 3D microfluidic paper‐based analytical device (3D-μPAD) is applied successfully for visually distinguishing three antibiotics, which shows great potential and versatility of M-GQDs for food safety monitoring.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Graphene quantum dots
Antibiotics
Visual differentiation
Determination
3D microfluidic paper‐based analytical device

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Fluorescence detection of TCs, AFQs and OFQs
Fluorescence distinction of TCs, AFQs and OFQs based on M-GQDs
Performance of TCs, AFQs and OFQs detection
Distinguishment and detection of DOX, PEF and LEV on 3D-μPAD platform

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: K C Wong Magna Fund in Ningbo University

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: National Natural Science Foundation of China
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by projects from National Natural Science Foundation of China under Grant (No. 61604084 ) and K. C. Wong Magna Fund in Ningbo University . The authors declare no competing financial interest.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic diagram of the preparation process and applications of M-GQDs.

(a) TEM image of M-GQDs (Inset shows the size distribution of M-GQDs). (b) HR-TEM image of M-GQDs. (c) AFM image of M-GQDs. (d) XRD pattern of M-GQDs. (e) Raman spectrum of M-GQDs. (f) FT-IR spectrum of M-GQDs. High-resolution XPS spectrum of N 1s (g), F 1s (h) and P 2p (i).

(a) The UV–vis absorption, PLE and PL spectra of M-GQDs. (b) PL spectra of M-GQDs under different excitation wavelengths (305.0–355.0 nm). (c) The two-dimensional map of M-GQDs. The fluorescence stability of M-GQDs under different storage time in daylight (d), ultraviolet light (e) and concentrations of NaCl aqueous solution (f).

(a) Normalized intensity of M-GQDs coexisting with different interfering species. Fluorescence spectra of M-GQDs with TCs (b), AFQs (c) and OFQs (d). (e) The UV–vis absorption spectra of M-GQDs with and without DOX, PEF and LEV. (f) The PLE and PL spectra of M-GQDs, UV–vis absorption spectrum of DOX. (g) FT-IR spectra of M-GQDs with PEF/LEV. (h) High-resolution O 1s spectra of M-GQDs with PEF and LEV. (i) The time-resolved PL spectra of M-GQDs in the absence and presence of LEV monitored at 498.0 nm and 419.0 nm. (j) Illustration of mechanisms for distinguishing TCs, AFQs and OFQs based on M-GQDs.

Fluorescence spectra of M-GQDs with different concentrations of DOX (a), PEF (d) and LEV (g). Images of M-GQDs with various concentrations of DOX (b), PEF (e) and LEV (h) under the excitation of sunlight and UV light. The relationships between fluorescence signal and the concentration of DOX (c), PEF (f) and LEV (i). Insets: the corresponding linear relationships between fluorescence signal and target concentration.

(a) Schematic representation of 3D-μPAD sensing platform. (b) Automated solution flow on the 3D-μPAD sensing platform. (c) Output RGB values of test zones with different DOX/PEF/LEV concentrations and the relationship between numerical signals and the concentration of targets.

head.ce:title: Construction of fluorescent sensor array and three-dimensional microfluidic paper based analytical device for specific identification and visual determination of antibiotics in food

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Microelectronic Science and Engineering, School of Physical Science and Technology, Ningbo University, Ningbo 315211, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Microelectronic Science and Engineering
School of Physical Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Microelectronic Science and Engineering, School of Physical Science and Technology, Ningbo University, Ningbo 315211, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Materials Science and Engineering, Shenzhen Key Laboratory of Full Spectral Solar Electricity Generation (FSSEG), Southern University of Science and Technology, Shenzhen 518055, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Materials Science and Engineering
Shenzhen Key Laboratory of Full Spectral Solar Electricity Generation (FSSEG)
Southern University of Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Materials Science and Engineering, Shenzhen Key Laboratory of Full Spectral Solar Electricity Generation (FSSEG), Southern University of Science and Technology, Shenzhen 518055, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: For antibiotics misuse since the global outbreak of COVID 19, a novel strategy for discriminating and detecting antibiotics is proposed based on the graphene quantum dots with multi-doped heteroatoms including F, N and P (M-GQDs), which exhibit blue emission (419.0 nm) under the excitation of 336.0 nm. Specifically, the fluorescence of M-GQDs is quenched by tetracyclines (TCs) owing to inner filter effect (IFE) and enhanced by alkane-modified fluoroquinolones (AFQs), which is attributed to restricted conformational rotation based on π-π stacking, hydrogen-bonding and electrostatic interactions. Meanwhile, the electron-accepting property of oxazine ring in oxazine-modified fluoroquinolones (OFQs) increases emission peak at 498.0 nm and decreases emission peak at 419.0 nm as the color changes from blue to cyan. Moreover, a cascade system integrated with 3D microfluidic paper‐based analytical device (3D-μPAD) is applied successfully for visually distinguishing three antibiotics, which shows great potential and versatility of M-GQDs for food safety monitoring.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Since the outbreak of COVID-19, the epidemic of COVID-19 around the globe has been followed by increased consumption of antibiotics (Kang, Chen, Chen, Tian, Wu, Zheng, & Li, 2022; Brahma, Naik, & Lordan, 2022). Antibiotics, capable of killing or interfering with the developmental functions of bacteria to fight bacterial infections, are well-known multifunctional antibacterial drugs. With the pressure of repurposing drugs to treat diseases caused by COVID-19, the situation of indiscriminately using antibiotics in COVID-19 treatment is more serious (Malik, & Mundra, 2023). Besides, the majority of the antibiotics eventually find their way into the environment and may accumulate in the human body through animal-derived foods, including meat, eggs, aquatic products, milk or dairy products, which can cause severe adverse reactions such as allergies, visual impairment and brain damage (Xu, Qing, Jiang, Zhang, & Feng, 2021). According to the chemical structure characteristics, the antibiotics can be classified into different categories, such as β-lactams, macrolides (MAs), sulfonamides (SAs), fluoroquinolones (FQs) and tetracyclines (TCs), among which TCs and FQs are the most common. TCs mainly include tetracycline (TC), chlortetracycline (CTC), oxytetracycline (OTC) and doxycycline (DOX). FQs can be divided into two categories according to the structure: alkane-modified fluoroquinolones (AFQs, including enrofloxacin (ENR), ciprofloxacin (CIP), pefloxacin (PEF) and fleroxacin (FLE)) and oxazine-modified fluoroquinolones (OFQs, including marbofloxacin (MAR), ofloxacin (OFL) and levofloxacin (LEV)) (Yang et al., 2021). Due to the diverse half-lifetimes and degradation strategies of different antibiotics in the environment, confusion of antibiotics pollutants has caused great difficulties for environmental remediation (Jia, Xu, Chen, Chen, & Xu, 2022). Therefore, the simultaneous differentiation and rapid detection of multiple antibiotics in complex environments can not only effectively reflect the environmental toxicology status and pollutant kinds in real time, but also provide technical support for pollution source control, environmental planning and public health protection.

To date, various analytical strategies have been used for monitoring antibiotics, including high-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC), spectrophotometry, capillary electrophoresis, colorimetric analysis and so on (Seraissol et al., 2022; Wang, Xia, & Gao, 2022; Aihaiti et al., 2022). However, the above traditional methods exhibit unavoidable defects, such as high cost of acquisition and maintenance, complicated operations and time-consuming real-time detection, which limit their application in real-time field analysis of the samples (Wang, Liu, Wang, Zhou, You, & Xiao, 2022). With the development and improvement of detection methods, fluorescent probes have been designed based on the fluorescence change between fluorescence groups and analytical targets, which are considered as the most promising one owing to their simple operation, facile manipulation, excellent analytical performance and good selectivity (Wang, Qiu, Kong, Hu, Zhong, & Tan, 2022; Hong et al., 2022). Meanwhile, graphene quantum dots (GQDs), a new type of zero-dimensional luminescent nanoparticles, have drawn much attention in recent years due to their exceptional properties including tunable photoluminescence, photostability, easy surface modification, superior solubility and low toxicity (Mukherjee, Das, Kundu, & Mandal, 2022; Wang et al., 2021). These outstanding photoluminescence properties facilitate the promising applications of GQDs in various fields, especially in fluorescence sensing (Wen et al., 2021; Li, Liu, Qi, & Liu, 2022). In recent years, numerous fluorescence probes have been proposed for detecting TCs or FQs based on fluorescence change between GQDs and targets. For example, Fan et al. prepared S, N-doped GQDs to detect TCs based on fluorescence quenching caused by the inner filter effect (IFE) (Fan, Qiao, Long, Chen, Fu, Zhou, & She, 2022). Kundu et al. developed GQDs for the quantitative detection of FQs on the basis of fluorescence enhancement (Kundu, Maity, & Basu, 2022). However, most fluorescent methods only respond to one single kind of antibiotics rather than distinguish multiple antibiotics, leading to the inaccurate investigation of antibiotics. Meanwhile, current fluorescence analysis relies on sophisticated equipment and the environmental requirements of darkness, which has largely limited the widespread application of GQDs (Rong et al., 2019). Therefore, it is significant to propose a simple, real-time, portable and precise fluorescence sensing platform for recognizing and determining various antibiotics.

Herein, we have proposed a novel fluorescent probe for discriminating and monitoring TCs, AFQs and OFQs based on prepared multi-doped GQDs (M-GQDs) with fluorine, nitrogen and phosphorus atoms, as shown in Fig. 1 . The M-GQDs are synthesized through a one-step hydrothermal way using norfloxacin (NOR) and phosphoric acid (PA) as precursors and exhibit strong blue emission (419.0 nm) with excellent stability, laying the foundation for the convenient differentiation and sensitive monitoring of TCs, AFQs and OFQs. Specifically, the fluorescence of M-GQDs is quenched by TCs due to the occurrence of IFE. Meanwhile, the introduction of AFQs induces a red shift and enhancement for M-GQDs fluorescence owing to restricted conformational rotation based on π-π stacking, hydrogen-bonding and electrostatic interactions. But dual emission peaks are observed after adding OFQs, which is attributed to electron transfer (ET) caused by distinctive appearance of oxazine rings in the structural formula. Here, DOX, PEF and LEV are chosen as the typical detecting models for TCs, AFQs and OFQs, respectively. The quantitative evaluation is established, covering from 5.000 to 50.00 µM for DOX, 3.000 to 30.00 µM for PEF and LEV with the limits of detection (LOD) of 39.30 nM, 48.60 nM and 35.10 nM, respectively. Furthermore, based on high accuracy and response diversity of M-GQDs, a 3D-multidimensional microfluidics paper-based analytical device (3D-µPAD) is developed for the simultaneous detection and differentiation of TCs, AFQs and OFQs, which paves the way for real-time detection and discrimination with higher sensitivity, and increases the outreach of fluorescence sensing.

NOR, ENR, CIP, PEF, FLE, MAR, OFL, LEV, TC, OTC, CTC, DOX, alanine (Ala), l-Asparagine (Asn), l-Cysteine (Cys), leucine (Leu), proline (Pro), serine (Ser), tryptophan (Trp), AlCl3, FeCl2, CoCl2, HgCl2, CdCl2, ZnCl2, CuCl2, PbCl2, MgCl2, CaCl2, AgCl, NaCl, KCl, NaOH (≥96.00%), H2SO4 (≥96.00%), PA and other chemicals were obtained from Aladdin Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China) and were used as received without further purification. Deionized water was deionized and purified with a resistivity of 18.25 MΩ∙cm by being passed through a Milli-Q and used in all experiments. Whatman no. 4 filter paper (205.0 μm thick) was used in microchannel assays and obtained from GE Healthcare Life Sciences (Beijing, China). Oil-based marker was the product of Shanghai Lemei Stationery Company (Shanghai, China).

M-GQDs were synthesized using a hydrothermal bottom-up approach (Cheng et al., 2022). First, phosphoric acid (1.000 mL) and NOR (1.000 mmol) were accurately weighted and dissolved in 25.00 mL of deionized water, and then performed ultrasonic treatment for 3.000 min to dissolve it. The solution was then transferred to a 100.0 mL polytetrafluoroethylene lined reactor, sealed and heated at 180.0 °C for 8.000 h (Wang et al., 2023). After the reaction was over, the reactor was cooled naturally to room temperature. Subsequently, the obtained solution was filtered through a 0.2200 µm membrane and dialyzed in deionized water for 48.00 h in a dialysis bag with a MWCO of 500.0 Da to remove the impurities of small molecules. Finally, the pale-yellow M-GQDs solution was obtained and prepared at 4.000 °C for further experiments.

A JEM-2100F Transmission electron microscope (TEM) was used to examine the morphologies of M-GQDs. Atomic force microscopy (AFM) images were taken with Cypher S (Oxford Instruments). The XRD pattern was measured by an X-ray diffraction (PANalytical X’Pert Pro MPD). Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR) was measured using a Nexus 470 (Thermo Fisher) spectrometer and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) of M-GQDs was performed on a PHI1580EXCA photoelectron spectrometer. Zeta potential was measured on a Zetasizer (Nano-Z, Malvern, U.K.). The optical ultraviolet–visible absorption spectra of M-GQDs (UV–vis) were recorded on UV-5800 spectrophotometer. The photoluminescence spectra (PL) and photoluminescence excitation spectra (PLE) were obtained using PerkinElmer LS55 luminescence spectrometer (PerkinElmer Instruments, U.K.).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence detection of TCs, AFQs and OFQs
ce:section.ce:para: DOX, PEF and LEV were chosen as the model targets for TCs, AFQs and OFQs, respectively. All the experimental methods and conditions were the same as follows. The target solution was prepared in deionized water with different concentrations and mixed with the M-GQDs solution in a ratio of 1.000:1.000 to obtain the mixer solution with a total volume of 3.000 mL. The fluorescence emission spectra were recorded after reaction for 1.000 min at room temperature, with the excitation wavelength of 336.0 nm. Similarly, various kinds of interferents (including metal ions, amino acids and small organic molecules) were introduced to measure the selectivity of the probe to model targets.

The 3D-µPAD platform consisted of three layers, which were prepared using non-fluorescence paper as the substrate of each layer. The hydrophobic hollow barrier on each layer was constructed through plotting specific patterns using the commercially obtained black oil-based marker. The top layer had three sets of hydrophilic zones, including sample zones, test zones and M-GQDs zone. The sample zones and M-GQDs zone were designed as circle areas with the diameter of 18.00 cm and 26.00 mm, respectively. Test zones were designed in a rectangular shape with 18.00 mm × 8.000 mm (detail information of the design of 3D-µPAD is expressed in Supporting information). On the second layer, three sets of hydrophilic channels were designed to transport samples solution to the test zones by capillary wicking forces of paper. Similarly, the bottom layer had the same three sets of hydrophilic channels for flowing M-GQDs solution. The sample and M-GQDs were added to the fluidic inlets and spread to the adjacent layers along the designed route. The other areas without liquid circulation were separated by plastic sheeting to prevent penetration between adjacent layers. The perpendicular overlap of adjacent paper layers provided a 3D fluid channel for transporting fluids to the detection zone. After sample solutions are added into the sample zones for 30.00 s, the sample and M-GQDs functioned completely after penetrating into the reaction zone. Stand for 5.000 min to make the liquids mix and react fully and evenly. The fluorescence images of test zones were recorded by smartphones under the UV light.

Different kinds of real samples such as milk, tap water and meat leachate were validate the practicability of 3D-μPAD. Milk and pork were purchased randomly from a local market (Ningbo, China). The tap water was collected from the campus of Ningbo University. Before the test, 5.000 g of pork was smashed and placed into 100.0 mL centrifuge tube and 50.00 mL of water was added into the tube. After mixing well, the meat leachate was obtained. All liquid samples (milk, tap water and meat leachate samples with a volume of 20.00 mL) were filtered using microfiltration membrane, and centrifuged (8000 r/min, 10.00 min) out the residues for all liquid samples. The obtained samples were left standing at 4.000 °C for 12.00 h, and the upper layer of the static solution was taken as the analysis solution. Add different concentrations of DOX, PEF or LEV to all the liquid samples, respectively. Then the experiments were run after equilibration for 1.000 min. Meanwhile, HPLC, as a reference method, was performed on the above samples, and the corresponding result of the t-test was analyzed.

The morphology and molecular structure of M-GQDs have been characterized. As displayed in Fig. 2 (a), M-GQDs are nearly spherical and uniformly dispersed in aqueous solution with the average diameter of 6.200 nm. The clear crystal lattice stripe can be observed from the high-resolution TEM (HR-TEM) image in Fig. 2(b) and the lattice spacing is 0.2400 nm, corresponding to the (100) planes of graphite carbon (Hsieh, Kao, Gandomi, Juang, Change, & Zhang, 2022). The AFM observation in Fig. 2(c) reveals that the thickness of M-GQDs is around 1.230 nm, suggesting 1.000–3.000 layers of graphene (Facure, Schneider, Mercante, & Correa, 2022). XRD pattern demonstrates that M-GQDs have two apparent peaks at approximately 2θ = 24.70° and 2θ = 43.20°, corresponding to (002) and (100) lattice spacing of carbon-based materials (Fig. 2(d)) (Hu et al., 2022). In the Raman spectrum of M-GQDs (Fig. 2(e)), two obvious peaks are observed at 1355 cM-1 (D) and 1574 cM-1 (G), corresponding to areas of disorder or defects and sp2 carbon networks in the carbon materials, respectively (Salee, Saud, Munira, Goh, Ismail, Siddiqui, & Zaidi, 2022). The calculated intensity ratio (ID/IG) of M-GQDs is obtained as 0.8400, indicating the high crystallinity of M-GQDs. The FT-IR spectrum is applied to further show the surface functional groups of M-GQDs. In Fig. 2(f), the broad absorption peaks at 3340 cm−1 and 2830 cm−1 are assigned to OH and CH stretching, respectively (Cai et al., 2022). The characteristic bands at approximately 1717 cm−1 and 1631 cm−1 correspond to the stretching vibrations of CO and CN, respectively. Peaks at 1464 cm−1 and 1253 cm−1 are ascribed to the stretching vibrations of CC and CN, respectively (Zhao, Zhang, Liu, Zhang, & Xiao, 2021). Furthermore, peaks at 1071 cm−1 and 823.0 cm−1, ascribed to C-F stretching and P-OH stretching, reveal the successful doping of F and P (Zeng et al., 2022; Pan et al., 2022). Besides, the XPS spectra are also analyzed to explore the surface state and the chemical components of prepared M-GQDs. The full XPS scan spectrum shows six peaks at 283.4 eV, 397.7 eV, 528.6 eV, 685.3 eV, 191.2 eV and 132.3 eV in Fig. S1(a), corresponding to the existence of C 1s, N 1s, O 1s, F 1s, P 2s and P 2p, respectively. Among the C 1s XPS spectrum, CO/CN (284.1 eV), CC/CC (285.2 eV) and C-F (288.6 eV) can be observed in Fig. S1(b) (Wang, Gao, Xu, Xu, Chen, Mei, & Xu, 2021). The N 1s spectrum (Fig. 2(g)) can be fitted into two components assigned to CN (399.0 eV) and NH (400.2 eV). The deconvoluted F 1s spectrum in Fig. 2(h) suggests the existence of semi-ionic C-F bond and covalent C-F at the peak of 684.4 eV and 686.8 eV (Zeng et al., 2022). Two peaks in the spectrum of P 2p (Fig. 2(i)) represent the PC/PN and PO bonds at 131.3 eV and 133.2 eV (Pan et al., 2022). Taken together, it can be concluded that the F, N and P atom have been successfully doped into M-GQDs, generating more defects and surface states to regulate the optical and photoelectric properties of M-GQDs. In Fig. S1(c), the O 1s XPS spectrum exhibits three peaks at 530.8 eV, 532.9 eV and 535.9 eV, corresponding to CO, CO and COC, respectively (Zhao, Zhang, Liu, Zhang, & Xiao, 2021).

The optical properties of the M-GQDs are studied through UV–vis absorption, excitation and emission spectra. As shown in Fig. 3 (a), two noticeable peaks can be observed at 262.0 nm and 319.0 nm in the UV–vis absorption spectrum (green line), which can be attributed to π-π* transition of CC band and n-π* transition of CO band, respectively (Li, Liu, Qi, & Liu, 2022). The optimal excitation and emission wavelength of M-GQDs are located at 336.0 nm and 419.0 nm. Meanwhile, the emission wavelength of M-GQDs presents the excitation-independent fluorescence phenomenon under different excitation ranging from 305.0 to 355.0 nm (Fig. 3(b)). The excitation-emission map of M-GQDs is shown in Fig. 3(c) with a small fluorescence area (λex = 336.0 nm, λem = 419.0 nm).

For further applications based on fluorescence probes, the optical stabilities of M-GQDs are investigated. In Fig. 3(d), the influence of storage time is studied first, and constant fluorescence intensity indicates excellent storability. Meanwhile, the fluorescence behavior of M-GQDs is impervious to ultraviolet radiation (Fig. 3(e)) and ion concentration (Fig. 3(f)), where M-GQDs are excited under UV light (365.0 nm) for 12.00 h, and the concentration of NaCl is varied from 0 to 2.000 mol/L. These results are indicative of good stabilities of M-GQDs under different conditions.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence distinction of TCs, AFQs and OFQs based on M-GQDs
ce:section.ce:para: The fluorescence behavior of GQDs has been widely used for detecting various substances based on the fluorescence changes, which are usually induced by the interaction between GQDs and targets. Considering the richness of functional groups on M-GQDs surfaces, interfering substances have been introduced to explore the specific identification of M-GQDs, including metal ions, amino acids and antibiotics. With the reference of the blank M-GQDs solution, the specific change of fluorescence phenomenon can be obviously observed upon the addition of different antibiotics in Fig. 4 (a), revealing the promise of M-GQDs to achieve a one-step distinction between similar antibiotics. As presented in Fig. 4(b), the introduction of TCs results in the fluorescence quenching of M-GQDs, and the emission remains at 419.0 nm. Fluorescence enhancement is recorded in Fig. 4(c) with a red shift after adding AFQs. Meanwhile, another emission at 498.0 nm increases, and fluorescence intensity at 419.0 nm decreases with the presence of OFQs in M-GQDs solution (Fig. 4(d)). The fluorescence spectra and images of M-GQDs with various antibiotics are shown in Fig. S2 and indicate a great potential to distinguish different types of antibiotics.

To understand the discriminating principle, DOX, PEF and LEV are taken as representatives of TCs, AFQs and OFQs respectively, to investigate the interaction between M-GQDs and targets. As shown in Fig. 4(e), the absorption peak of M-GQDs has no pronounced shift in the presence of DOX. Nevertheless, an apparent red/blue shift can be observed on the absorption peak of M-GQDs at 262.0 nm after adding PEF/LEV, indicating the change of the electron cloud density in the sp2 core, which may be attributed to the binding interaction between M-GQDs and targets (Moncomble, Falantin, & Cornard, 2018). The binding constant (K), reflecting the interaction strength between M-GQDs and targets, is calculated to confirm the binding action between M-GQDs and targets through the Benesi-Hildebrand equation (Yang, Jia, Hou, Zhao, & Wang, 2022):
(1)
1
F -
F 0
=
1
K
F s
-
F 0
C
+
1
F s
-
F 0
where F0 and F are fluorescence intensities of M-GQDs in the absence and presence of an analyte, Fs and C represent the fluorescence intensity of saturated analyte and the concentration of analyte, respectively. As presented in Fig. S3, the K value of M-GQDs with DOX (1.2 × 104 mol−1) is lower than that of M-GQDs with PEF (3.1 × 104 mol−1) and LEV (2.3 × 104 mol−1), illustrating that more intense interactions exist between M-GQDs and FQs.

It is well known that IFE is a major reason for explaining the fluorescence quenching of fluorophores, which results from an attenuation of the excitation beam or absorption of the emitted radiation by the quencher in solution (Revesz, Hickey, & Sweetman, 2022). As shown in Fig. 4(f), there is an apparent overlap between the optimal excitation spectrum of M-GQDs and the UV–vis absorption spectrum of DOX, indicating that the competitive energy absorption between M-GQDs and DOX results in less excitation and fluorescence quenching of M-GQDs. To further confirm the IFE between M-GQDs and DOX, the fluorescence attenuation curves of M-GQDs with and without DOX are displayed in Fig. S4. The almost complete overlap of the two curves manifests the occurrence of IFE between M-GQDs and DOX, where average lifetime values of M-GQDs with and without DOX have barely change (Zheng, Wang, Li, Pan, Li, Qi, & Li, 2021).

Compared with DOX, fluorescence behaviors different from quenching are observed with the addition of PEF/LEV, which are related to severe interaction reflected by larger values of K. Further studies based on FT-IR, XPS and zeta potential are analyzed to elucidate mechanisms between M-GQDs and PEF/LEV. Fig. 4(g) shows FT-IR spectra of M-GQDs with PEF (green line)/LEV (orange line). Compared with the M-GQDs baseline, the band of OH/NH stretching vibration (at 3000–3500 cm−1) broadens with blue shifts for the absorption peaks of C-F (1013 cm−1) and CO (1639 cm−1), demonstrating the hydrogen-bonding interaction of M-GQDs and PEF/LEV (Lin, Yang, Wang, Cui, Li, Chen, & Ding, 2022). Besides, the increasing of C/O contents (Fig. S5 and Table S1) and the portions of CO (Fig. 4(h) and Table S2) obtained through XPS analysis further verify the existence of π-π stacking and hydrogen-bonding interaction between M-GQDs and targets (Lee, Hu, & Chiu, 2022).

Moreover, the electrostatic interaction is also speculated as the origin of fluorescence change, and zeta potential measurement is additionally performed in Fig. S6. The zeta potential of −18.54 mV for M-GQDs suggests that their surfaces are negatively charged. However, it is found that the zeta potential of M-GQDs increases to −8.930 mV with the addition of LEV and drastically increases to −4.670 mV with the addition of PEF. The drastic change reveals a strong electrostatic interaction between M-GQDs and PEF than that of M-GQDs with LEV, which is in accordance with the value of K. Therefore, the strong interaction between M-GQDs and PEF can induce the occurrence of complexation, and restrict their intramolecular vibrations and rotations, which are responsible for the inhibition of the nonradiative process (NR) and the enhancement of the radiation transition.

Although LEV also has π-π stacking and hydrogen-bonding interaction with M-GQDs, the addition of LEV presents the phenomenon of double emission peaks, which is different from the enhancing an emission with PEF. Considering the structural characteristics of LEV, the appearance of emission peak at 498.0 nm can be assigned to the energy transfer (ET) between M-GQDs and LEV. Oxazine ring, as the characteristic structure of OFQs (Fig. S7), has been reported as a photoexcited electron-accepting group. It drives electrons to transfer from M-GQDs to LEV by the channel provided by the π-π stacking and hydrogen-bonding interaction between them (Kobzar, Tkachenko, Bliznyuk, Lobko, Shekera, & Shevchenko, 2018). The addition of LEV induces the occurrence of new energy levels between the electron donor (M-GQDs) and the electron acceptor (LEV), causing the fluorescence quenching of emission at 419.0 nm and the increasing intensity at 498.0 nm. The fluorescence decaying curves of M-GQDs monitored at 498.0 nm and 419.0 nm in the presence and absence of LEV are measured in Fig. 4(i), which can be well fitted by the following double-exponential equation (Wang, He, Xu, Sui, Jiang, Ran, & Song, 2022):
(2)
I
t
=
C 1
e x p
- t / τ
1
+
C 2
e x p
- t / τ
2
where τ 1 and τ 2 are decay time constants, and C 1 and C 2 are the corresponding fitting parameters. Double exponential decay lifetime values indicate that electrons have two distinct radiative transition channels, corresponding to the recombination of the surface state and molecular state (Zhao, Ma, Wang, Yang, Li, Qiu, & Xiao, 2019). With the introduction of LEV, the weight of fast decay coefficient (τ 1 = 2.640 ns) decreases from 48.46% to 8.040% and the τ 2 (6.61 ns) weight increases from 51.54% to 91.96%, verifying that extensive electrons are transferred from M-GQDs and captured by LEV, which produces radiative recombination at the low energy level for the generation of new fluorescence emission at 498.0 nm. The increase of average fluorescence lifetime (from 4.670 ns to 6.420 ns) also indicates the electron transfer process between M-GQDs and LEV. Meanwhile, τ 1 weight decreases from 23.16% to 15.60% and τ 2 weight increases from 76.84% to 84.40% at 419.0 nm, further validating the electron transfer from M-GQDs to LEV (Table S3) (Wang, Deng, Yang, Chen, Long, She, & Fu, 2022).

To sum up, different sensing mechanisms exist for varying degrees of interaction when various antibiotics are added. As presented in Fig. 4(j), IFE dominates the quenching of M-GQDs with DOX, while strong interaction causes different fluorescence behavior of M-GQDs in the presence of PEF and LEV. The addition of PEF results in restricted conformational rotation to activate the fluorescence intensity of M-GQDs based on π-π stacking, hydrogen-bonding and electrostatic interactions. The existence of the oxazine ring in OFQs prompts the occurrence of ET process, leading to the quenching of M-GQDs and increasing of LEV.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Performance of TCs, AFQs and OFQs detection
ce:section.ce:para: Given the coexisting conditions of complex substance, the anti-interference ability of the proposed fluorescence probe is evaluated for further application of M-GQDs. Briefly, the interfering effect of various substance are explored in Fig. S8. As a result, these molecules and ions can rarely trigger any significant difference during the detection processes, demonstrating the excellent capacity of resisting disturbance towards routine interference.

Further, the sensing ability of M-GQDs for antibiotics is discussed through fluorescence spectroscopy. By virtue of M-GQDs responding differentially to three kinds of antibiotics, the fluorescence emission of mixtures of M-GQDs and specific antibiotics is measured. Fig. 5 (a) shows that the fluorescence intensity of M-GQDs gradually decreases with the addition of DOX and the dimming blue fluorescence can be observed in Fig. 5(b). Simultaneously, a plot of normalized intensity (F/F0 ) against DOX concentration is shown in Fig. 5(c), where a good linear correlation is obtained in the range of 5.000–50.00 μM with a correlation coefficient (R 2) of 0.9983. The calculated LOD is 39.30 nM (S/N = 3.000). Similarly, the fluorescence spectra of M-GQDs with various concentrations of PEF are recorded under the same conditions. As shown in Fig. 5(d), the emission of M-GQDs has a red-shift from 419.0 to 441.0 nm and an obvious fluorescence enhancement is observed, which is consistent with the fluorescence change under UV lamps (Fig. 5(e)). F/F 0 ratio of M-GQDs increases with the concentration of PEF ranging from 3.000 to 200.0 μM (Fig. 5(f)), and the LOD of 48.60 nM is obtained in the linear region from 3.000 to 30.00 μM (Fig. 5(f) inset). Besides, a ratiometric fluorescence sensor is constructed to detect LEV based on the enhancement of emission peak at 498.0 nm and fluorescence quenching at 419.0 nm with the addition of LEV (Fig. 5(g)). The obvious color change from blue to cyan is recorded in Fig. 5(h). The fluorescence signal (F 498/F 419) is positively correlated with LEV concentration in the linear interval of 3.000–30.00 μM (R 2 = 0.9993) and the LOD of 35.10 nM (Fig. 5(i)). In addition, the sensing performance of the M-GQDs-based fluorescence probe is comparable to other works in terms of LOD (Table S4), reflecting the excellent detection limit of antibiotics detection.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Distinguishment and detection of DOX, PEF and LEV on 3D-μPAD platform
ce:section.ce:para: The above results confirm that different types and concentrations of antibiotics could be accurately and quantitatively recognized using the fluorescence change of M-GQDs. However, the methods are always limited by using specific equipment, which cannot satisfy the growing need for real-time monitoring antibiotics. Thus, the development of devices with portability and high accuracy is necessary and urgent. With this in mind, the 3D-μPAD platform has been proposed to realize convenient, visual and accurate discrimination and detection of antibiotics, which combine with the convenience of test paper and the data analysis capabilities of smartphones. As shown in Fig. 6 (a), the whole 3D-μPAD consists of three layers of non-fluorescent paper with the shape of rectangle. The top layer is used to construct three sets of sample zones, test zones and M-GQDs zone. Three sets of hydrophilic channels are designed on the second layer to transport sample solution to the test zones through the capillary wicking force of paper. Similarly, the bottom layer has the same three sets of hydrophilic channels for the flowing M-GQDs solution. The sample and M-GQDs solution are added to the fluidic inlets and spread to adjacent contact paper layers. The hydrophilic channels on each layer transport water sample by capillary wicking forces of paper and the overlapping areas of adjacent papers in the vertical space provide the 3D fluidic channel for the flow of fluid at different layers. When all solutions are mixed in the test zones, fluorescence color changes can be presented on the top layer. Fluorescence images presented under ultraviolet irradiation are captured and uploaded by smartphones to obtain corresponding RGB values as sensing signals.

After dropwise adding sample solutions, the flow of fluid through hydrophilic channels on each layer is shown as Fig. 6(b), further demonstrating the feasibility of 3D-μPAD application in real-time monitoring. The fluorescence zone soaked in the as-prepared M-GQDs droplet shows blue fluorescence under UV light. In contrast, the text zones soaked in M-GQDs with DOX, PEF and LEV present weak blue, bright blue and cyan, respectively. Fluorescence photos of text zones introduced with other antibiotics are presented in Fig. S9. Distinct color changes of test zones yield great potential for visually distinguishing TCs, AFQs or OFQs. In addition, the sensing performance of 3D-μPAD is evaluated by extracting RGB values of fluorescent images. The photographs of 3D-μPAD with different concentrations of targets are recorded (Fig. S10), of which corresponding RGB values are analyzed through app software for three times. As presented in Fig. 6(c), by converting the above color signals into numerical signals, RGB values has clear linear correlations with concentrations of target compounds in certain range, 0–50.00 μmol/L (R 2 = 0.9910) for DOX, 0–50.00 μmol/L (R 2 = 0.9945) for PEF and 0–20.00 μmol/L (R 2 = 0.9918) for LEV. The LODs of DOX, PEF and LEF are obtained as 97.10 nM, 97.30 nM and 107.1 nM, respectively.

Antibiotics, as a typical kind of antibacterial drug and feed additive, are easy to infiltrate into the environment and enriched in the animal-derived food product through the food chain, threatening more and more to people's lives and health. Thus, the proposed sensing platform is applied for the determination of antibiotics in real samples, including milk, tap water and meat leachate. Recovery experiments are carried out to verify its practical applicability using spiked samples of DOX/PEF/LEV with different concentrations in real samples and the analysis of t-test is executed when HPLC is chosen as a reference method (Table S5). It can be observed that the recovery values of targets in samples range from 97.30% to 105.3% with a relative standard deviation (%RSD) less than 6.720%. Moreover, the t-test result shows that there are no statistically significant differences being observed between the results of two methods. All results confirm the reliability and high accuracy of the proposed strategy based on 3D-μPAD, which provides a reliable alternative to current fluorescence sensing strategies for an easy, sensitive and convenient platform to discriminate and monitor antibiotics in real samples.

In summary, a novel fluorescence probe for distinguishing and determining diverse antibiotics is constructed based on M-GQDs, which are fabricated by a one-step hydrothermal method and exhibit blue emission at 419.0 nm. Specially, the fluorescence of M-GQDs is quenched by TCs due to the occurrence of IFE. The introduction of AFQs results in the fluorescence enhancement with red shift, which is attributed to the restricted conformational rotation based on drastic interaction between M-GQDs and AFQs. Meanwhile, the electron-accepting properties of the oxazine ring in oxazine-modified fluoroquinolones (OFQs) make two emission peaks exist at 419.0 nm and 498.0 nm, resulting in the color change from blue to cyan. Multifarious responses of M-GQDs towards antibiotics show the potential for visually distinguishing and detecting TCs, AFQs and OFQs. Moreover, a 3D-μPAD sensing platform is constructed and the smartphone is employed to analyze the concentration of antibiotics with favorable results, demonstrating the practical application and the potential of on-site detection. Thus, the strategy not only opens new avenues for utilizing M-GQDs in sensing applications but also presents broad application prospect for real-time on-site sensing in smart devices.

Tingting Li: Investigation, Formal analysis, Writing-original draft. Guoqiang Guo: Validation, Formal analysis. Haoming Xing: Visualization. Siyuan Tang: Project administration. Houwen Hu: Investigation. Linfan Wang: Investigation. Xiaoqing Qian: Writing-review & editing. Da Chen: Conceptualization, Methodology, Resources.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by projects from National Natural Science Foundation of China under Grant (No. 61604084) and K. C. Wong Magna Fund in Ningbo University. The authors declare no competing financial interest.

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.foodchem.2023.136947.
